questions.question_text: A property owner hires a friend to find a buyer for their home and offers a fee. Does the friend need an Idaho real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, compensation for brokerage services requires a license
distractors: No, friends and family are exempt from Idaho licensing
Only if the compensation exceeds $1,000 per transaction
Only if they advertise the property in public listings
questions.explanation: In Idaho, anyone who performs real estate brokerage activities for compensation must hold a license. There is no exemption for friends or family members receiving fees for brokerage services.

questions.question_text: Which activity would require an Idaho real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: Negotiating a sale of real property for another for compensation
distractors: Selling your own personal residence
An attorney handling a real estate closing
A bank employee processing mortgage loans
questions.explanation: Negotiating a sale of real property for another for compensation is a brokerage activity requiring licensure. Owners selling their own property, attorneys practicing law, and bank employees processing loans are generally exempt.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed individual manages rental properties for a landlord in exchange for reduced rent. Is this lawful in Idaho?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, property management is not a licensed activity in Idaho
distractors: No, property management for compensation requires a license
No, unless managing fewer than 5 units
Only if the landlord files written consent with IREC
questions.explanation: Idaho is one of the few states where property management is not a licensed activity — IREC itself notes that property management (leasing) is not regulated in Idaho. Leasing, renting, and collecting rent for others do not require a license. A license IS required for sales activity, including negotiating lease-options, lease-purchases, or rights of first refusal.

questions.question_text: Which person is EXEMPT from Idaho real estate licensing requirements?
questions.correct_answer: An owner selling their own property without an agent
distractors: A paid referral agent with no license
A person negotiating home sales for others for a fee
An unlicensed assistant negotiating offers
questions.explanation: Property owners selling their own real estate are exempt from licensing requirements. Paid referral agents, unlicensed assistants negotiating offers, and anyone negotiating sales for others for compensation must be licensed. (Property management, by contrast, is not a licensed activity in Idaho.)

questions.question_text: A retired licensee occasionally helps neighbors list their homes for a small fee. Does this require a license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, listing property for others for a fee requires a license
distractors: No, occasional activity is exempt from Idaho licensing
No, if the compensation fee is under $500 per deal
Only if more than 3 properties are listed per year
questions.explanation: Any person performing licensed real estate activities for compensation must hold an active license, regardless of frequency. There is no occasional activity exemption in Idaho.

questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education must an Idaho salesperson candidate complete?
distractors: 60 hours
75 hours
120 hours
questions.explanation: Idaho requires 90 hours of pre-license education divided into Module 1 (45 hours of fundamentals) and Module 2 (45 hours of applied skills).

questions.question_text: A salesperson candidate completed Module 1 three and a half years ago. Can they still use it toward their application?
questions.correct_answer: No, courses must be completed within 3 years of application
distractors: Yes, there is no time limit on pre-license courses
Yes, if they pass the state licensing exam afterward
No, pre-license courses expire after only 1 year
questions.explanation: All pre-license courses must be completed within the 3 years immediately preceding the license application date. Module 1 completed 3.5 years ago has expired and must be retaken.

questions.question_text: What minimum score must a salesperson candidate achieve on each pre-license course exam?
questions.explanation: Idaho requires a minimum passing score of 70% on each pre-license course final exam. This applies to both Module 1 and Module 2.

questions.question_text: Which is NOT an eligibility requirement for an Idaho salesperson license?
distractors: Age 18 or older
High school diploma or equivalent
Fingerprinting for background check
questions.explanation: Idaho requires applicants to be 18+, have a high school diploma or equivalent, and submit fingerprints for a background check. There is no college education requirement.

questions.question_text: Idaho salesperson pre-license education is divided into which two modules?
questions.correct_answer: Fundamentals (45 hrs) and Applied Skills (45 hrs)
distractors: Law (60 hrs) and Practice (30 hrs)
Principles (45 hrs) and Contracts (45 hrs)
National (50 hrs) and State (40 hrs)
questions.explanation: Idaho's 90-hour pre-license education consists of Module 1 (45 hours covering fundamental real estate concepts) and Module 2 (45 hours covering applied skills in listing, selling, buying, and closing).

questions.question_text: What minimum E&O insurance coverage must an individual Idaho licensee maintain?
distractors: $50,000 per claim / $100,000 aggregate
$250,000 per claim / $500,000 aggregate
$100,000 per claim / $100,000 aggregate
questions.explanation: Idaho requires individual licensees to carry at least $100,000 per claim and $300,000 aggregate E&O coverage. Companies must carry $500,000/$1,000,000.

questions.question_text: A salesperson lets their E&O insurance lapse for two weeks then renews it. What consequence may they face?
questions.correct_answer: Loss of prior acts coverage and possible license inactivation
distractors: No consequence if renewed within 30 days
Automatic license revocation
A warning letter with no penalties
questions.explanation: Even a one-day gap in E&O coverage results in loss of prior acts coverage. A licensee who fails to show proof of coverage within 30 days of Commission notice has their license inactivated (Idaho Code 54-2013), and discipline including fines is also possible.

questions.question_text: E&O insurance for an Idaho real estate company must provide minimum coverage of what amount?
distractors: $100,000 per claim / $300,000 aggregate
$250,000 per claim / $500,000 aggregate
$300,000 per claim / $600,000 aggregate
questions.explanation: Idaho real estate companies must carry E&O insurance of at least $500,000 per claim and $1,000,000 aggregate, which is higher than the individual licensee requirement of $100,000/$300,000.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Idaho E&O insurance is TRUE?
questions.correct_answer: All active licensees must maintain continuous coverage
distractors: Only brokers are required to carry E&O
E&O is optional if the broker provides bonding
Inactive licensees must also maintain E&O
questions.explanation: E&O insurance is mandatory for all actively licensed real estate professionals in Idaho. Inactive licensees do not need to maintain E&O since they cannot perform licensed activities.

questions.question_text: An Idaho licensee wants to obtain E&O independently rather than through the Commission's group policy. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the coverage meets minimum requirements
distractors: No, all licensees must use the Commission's policy
Yes, but only for broker licensees
No, independent policies are not recognized
questions.explanation: Licensees may obtain E&O insurance independently as long as the policy meets IREC's minimum coverage requirements ($100,000/$300,000 individual). The Commission also makes a group policy available that cannot cancel individual licensees.
